Your app already listens on a port and answers requests just fine — so why put Nginx in front of it?
If you’re deploying a web application for the first time, this question comes up naturally:
If my application is already listening on a port, why do I need Nginx at all?
It’s a fair question. A Node.js app can listen on port 3000, respond to HTTP requests, and serve users perfectly well on its own. If it already works, adding another piece of software in front of it can feel unnecessary.
The answer comes down to a distinction that’s easy to miss at first: serving an application and exposing it to the internet are two different responsibilities. Modern web infrastructure keeps them separate on purpose.
A Web Application Only Solves One Problem
Frameworks like Express, FastAPI, Flask, or Laravel exist to handle application logic — receiving requests, executing code, querying databases, and returning responses. That’s the job they’re built for.
They were never designed to also act as complete, internet-facing web servers responsible for SSL termination, request routing, traffic management, or efficiently serving static assets. Those responsibilities belong elsewhere, to tools purpose-built for them.
The Role of a Reverse Proxy
This is where Nginx comes in.
Rather than letting clients talk directly to the application, every request first arrives at Nginx, which decides what happens next. Sometimes it serves a static file. Sometimes it redirects the request. Sometimes it forwards traffic to another service running on the same machine.
That forwarding behavior is called reverse proxying. Instead of clients talking directly to the application, they talk to Nginx — and Nginx talks to the application on their behalf. The request flow looks like this:
Browser
│ HTTPS request
▼
Nginx ──reverse proxy──▶ Node.js application
▲ │
│ HTTPS response JSON response
└──────────────────────────────┘From the browser’s perspective, the entire website is Nginx. The application behind it is invisible to the client.
Keeping the Backend Private
Most Node.js applications listen on ports like 3000 or 5000. Rather than exposing those ports to the public internet, the application is typically configured to listen only on the loopback interface (127.0.0.1) — meaning it only accepts connections from the same machine. External users can't reach it directly; only Nginx can.
That’s a small architectural decision with a real security payoff: even if someone knows an app is running on port 3000, there’s no route from the internet to it. The only publicly reachable entry points are ports 80 and 443, both controlled by Nginx.
Why Nginx Handles HTTPS
HTTPS is another reason reverse proxies exist. Establishing encrypted TLS connections involves certificate management, protocol negotiation, and cryptographic overhead — and rather than teaching every application framework how to manage all of that, production environments typically let Nginx terminate TLS instead.
The browser establishes an encrypted HTTPS session with Nginx. Nginx decrypts the request and forwards a plain HTTP request to the backend application running locally. Since that internal hop never leaves the server, encrypting it adds little practical value. The application stays focused on processing requests instead of managing encryption it doesn’t need to know about.
Applications Shouldn’t Depend on Open Terminals
A common mistake when deploying a Node.js app for the first time is starting it with:
node app.jsThat works — until the terminal closes. Once the SSH session ends, so does the process, because production services can’t depend on someone keeping a terminal window open indefinitely.
This is where a service manager like systemd comes in. It starts applications automatically during system boot, monitors them while running, and restarts them if they unexpectedly terminate. From the operating system's perspective, a web application becomes just another managed service — no different from Nginx or the SSH daemon.
Separating Responsibilities
One of the most valuable lessons in infrastructure is that systems get easier to manage when each component has one clearly defined job:

• Nginx handles incoming traffic.

• The application handles business logic.

• The operating system manages processes.

• Git manages source code.
Each tool focuses on solving a single problem well. When responsibilities start to overlap, deployments become harder to maintain, debug, and scale.
The Bigger Picture
It’s tempting to think of deployment as simply copying files onto a server and starting a process. In reality, it’s about designing a system where every component knows its role: a browser requests a page, Nginx accepts the connection, the application generates a response, the operating system keeps that application alive, and certificates secure the whole exchange.
None of these pieces are particularly complicated on their own. Together, they form an architecture capable of reliably serving millions of requests.
Understanding how those pieces fit together is what turns deployment from a checklist into a discipline.

If this connected some dots for you — or you’d like to talk infrastructure, share ideas, or have an opportunity worth discussing — I’m on LinkedIn: Connect with me.
